content.full_text:
RAy! MEHTA
CHAIRMAN
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
May 20, 1997
Mr. Charles G. Abdelnour, J.D.
City Clerk, C.M.C.
City of San Diego
City Administration Building
202 C Street
San Diego, California 92101
Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-97-164
Dear Mr. Abdelnour:
This letter is a response to your request for advice regarding the provisions ofthe
Political Reform Act (the “Act”).’
QUESTIONS
1. To which members ofthe city council, including the mayor who is a voting member of
the council, may an appointee make campaign contributions?
2. Does the answer to Question 1 change regarding Councilmember X if Councilmember
X was absent, abstained, or voted no when the city council confirmed the appointment?
3. Does the answer to Question 1 change regarding Councilmember X if Councilmember
X nominated the appointee?
CONCLUSIONS
1. Ifthe mayor appoints an individual to a public board or commission, the individual
may not make a contribution to or solicit a contribution on behalf of the mayor’s campaign
committee. If the council appoints an individual to a public board or commission, the individual
may not make a contribution to or solicit a contribution on behalf of any ofthe councilmembers’
campaign committees.
Government Code sections 81000 - 91014. Commission regulations appear at title 2, sections 18109 -
18995, ofthe California Code of Regulations.
A’OTC’.____...
C’_fl(fl
._____
,,


File No. A-97-164
Page 2
2. No. If the entire council has the power to appoint and appoints an individual to a
public board or commission, the individual may not make a contribution to or solicit a
contribution on behalf of anyone who is a member of the council at the time ofthe vote. It is
irrelevant whether a particular councilmember voted yes, no, abstained or was absent during the
vote on the appointee.
3. If a councilmember submitted an individual’s name for nomination to a public board
or commission, but the mayor appointed the individual, the individual would only be prohibited
from making a contribution to or soliciting a contribution on behalf ofthe mayor under section
85705. The individual is not prohibited from making contributions to the councilmember who
submitted his or her name for consideration.
FACTS
Sections 41 and 432 ofthe San Diego City charter provide for the appointment process to
city commissions, advisory boards and committees essentially as follows: the mayor, who is a
voting member ofthe city council, makes the appointment, subject to the confirmation ofthe city
council. Whenever the mayor does not make an appointment within 45 days of a vacancy, the
city council makes the appointment. Additionally, the city council may remove appointees by
majority vote.
There are additional boards, not referenced in the charter, for which the council makes the
appointment. In at least one instance, the mayor makes appointments from a list ofnominations
submitted by each councilmember, and the council then confirms the appointments.
ANALYSIS
Proposition 208 enacted a new prohibition on contributions from governmental
appointees to the officeholders who appointed them. Section 85705 provides as follows:
“No person appointed to a public board or commission or as
Trustee ofthe California State University or Regent of the
University of California during tenure in office shall donate to, or
solicit or accept any campaign contribution for, any committee
controlled by the person who made the appointment to that office
or any other entity with the intent that the recipient ofthe donation
be any committee controlled by such person who made the
appointment.” (Emphasis added.)
2 Section 43 of the San Diego City charter which you have provided us authorizes the council to establish
advisory boards and committees. The Lyions Advice Letter, No. A-97-203, copy enclosed, discusses the application
of section 85305 to advisory boards or committees.


File No. A-97-164
Page 3
The Act defines “person” broadly to mean “an individual, proprietorship, firm,
partnership, joint venture, syndicate, business trust, company, corporation, limited liability
company, association, committee, and any other organization or group ofpersons acting in
concert.” (Section 82047.) Under this definition, “the person who made the appointment” in
section 85705 could be either the mayor or the city council.
You state that for certain city boards and commissions, the mayor, who is a voting
member of the council, makes the appointment, subject to the confirmation by the council. Ifthe
mayor does not make an appointment within 45 days of a vacancy, then the council makes the
appointment. For certain other boards, the council makes the appointment.
If the mayor appoints an individual to a public board or commission in Sari Diego, that
individual, for the duration of his or her appointment, may not make a campaign contribution to
the mayor’s campaign committee, or solicit or accept contributions on behalfofthe mayor’s
campaign committee. Just because a particular mayoral appointee is ratified or confirmed by the
council, however, does not mean that the appointee is prohibited from making campaign
contributions to councilmembers (excluding the mayor). We have advised that when the mayor
appoints an individual subject to the concurrence of the city council, only the mayor is the
“person who made the appointment” under section 85705. (Lyions Advice Letter, supra.)
Alternatively, ifthe entire council has the power to appoint and appoints an individual to
a public board or commission in San Diego, that individual, for the duration ofhis or her
appointment, may not make campaign contributions to any councilmember’s campaign
committee, or solicit or accept contributions on behalf of any councilmember’s campaign
committee. In this case, every member ofthe council at the time of the vote, including the mayor
who is a voting member ofthe council, is considered “the person who made the appointment”
under section 85705. Ifthe council has the authority to appoint, and does appoint, an individual
to a public board or commission, that individual is prohibited from making contributions to any
members ofthe council, including the mayor. It does not matter whether a particular
councilmember voted yes, voted no, abstained, or was absent during the vote to appoint the
individual.
With respect to one board or commission, you state that the mayor makes appointments
from a list ofnominations submitted by each councilmember, and the council then confirms the
appointments. Despite the fact that a councilmember may submit an individual’s name for
nomination to a public board or commission, the mayor has the power of appointment and
appoints the individual. Section 85705 only prohibits an individual from maldng a campaign
contribution to the controlled committee of “theperson who made the appointment.” The section
does not prohibit an individual from making a campaign contribution to the person who made the
nomination. In this case, the individual would only be prohibited from making a contribution to
or soliciting a contribution on behalf ofthe mayor who appointed him or her. The individual is
not prohibited from making contributions to the councilmember who submitted his or her name
for consideration.


File No. A-97-164
Page 4
Ifyou have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-
5660.
Sincerely,
Steven G. Churchwell
General Counsel
-
f2,
By: Hyla P. Wagner
Staff Counsel, Legal Division
Enclosure
SGC:HPW:ak

Ifyou have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-
